children.text: If any of the topics in this project interest you, I have listed a number of books by topic below if you would like to learn more.
The Chicano Movement in San Antonio
Quixote’s Soldiers: A Local History of the Chicano Movement, 1966-1981.
By David Montejano University of Texas Press, 2010.
Sancho’s Journal: Exploring the Political Edge with the Brown Berets.
By David Montejano. University of Texas Press, 2012.
“From Police Brutality to the ‘United People’s Party’: San Antonio’s Hybrid SNCC Chapter, the Chicano/a Movement, and Political Change.” Essay by Max Krochmal. In
Civil Rights in Black and Brown: Histories of Resistance and Struggle in Texas
, 259–85. University of Texas Press, 2021.
Mexican American Education
The Strange Career of Bilingual Education in Texas: 1836-1981.
By Carlos Kevin Blanton. Texas A&M University Press, 2004.
“We Want Better Education!” The 1960s Chicano Student Movement, School Walkouts, and the Quest for Educational Reform in South Texas.
By James B. Barrera. Texas A&M University Press, 2024.
Reading, Writing, and Revolution: Escuelitas and the Emergence of a Mexican American Identity in Texas.
By Phyllis M. Barragan Goetz. University of Texas Press, 2022.
Chicana Feminism
Making Face, Making Soul/Haciendo Caras: Creative and Critical Perspectives of Feminists of Color.
Gloria Anzaldúa. Aunt Lute Books, 1990.
Chicana Movidas: New Narratives of Activism and Feminism in the Movement Era.
Edited by Dionne Espinoza, María Eugenia Cotera, and Maylei Blackwell. University of Texas Press. 2019.
Fleshing the Archive: An Intimate Genealogy of Chicana Knowledge Practice.
By María Eugenia Cotera. University of Texas Press. 2026.
For an additional, written interview with Diana Montejano, see:
“Braiding Languages, Weaving Cultures: Conversation With Diana Montejano.” Essay by Nancy Sullivan in
Conversations with Mexican American Writers: Languages and Literatures in the Borderlands,
21–34. Jackson, MS: University Press of Mississippi, 2009.

This list is not exhaustive. There are many more works on these topics which are actively being produced and which already exist. Mexican American, Chicane, Women, Latine, and Education studies are flourishing fields whose scholars work hard to bring these stories to light. This project could not exist without them.
